title: Drumming Practices for Stuck Anger
dek: When the fury sits heavy in your chest and your jaw aches from holding it back, sometimes the only way out is through rhythm. Here is how to let your hands speak the language your throat forgot.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight in Your Hands


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.223Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:14 AM and the air in the room feels too thick to breathe. It is not sadness. It is not anxiety. It is the dense, vibrating hum of anger, trapped somewhere between your diaphragm and your throat. Your jaw is locked, the masseter muscles tight as coiled springs. Your hands are clenched into fists under the blankets, the knuckles white in the dark. You have been taught that this feeling is dangerous, that it is unspiritual, that it is a moral failure. But your body knows better. This is not pathology. This is biology.

Anger is a messenger, and right now, it is screaming. It is demanding to be felt, to be moved, to be acknowledged. And when words fail, when the throat closes up and the mind spins in circles, the body needs a different language. It needs rhythm. It needs the primal, undeniable strike of hand against skin, of wood against wood. It needs drumming.

When you strike a drum, you are not just making noise. You are moving energy. You are taking the stagnant, stuck fury that has been pooling in your psoas and your trapezius, and you are giving it a way out. You are letting your hands speak the language your throat forgot. The physical act of striking is a reclamation of your own power, a refusal to swallow the poison of unexpressed rage. It is a declaration that your body is a vessel for truth, not a storage unit for other people's transgressions.

## The Anatomy of Rhythm

Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. The trauma, the anger, the unspoken boundaries - they do not just vanish because we decide to be "high-vibrational" or because we repeat affirmations in the mirror. They lodge themselves in our fascia, in our muscles, in our nervous system. Van der Kolk emphasizes that healing requires moving the body, engaging the rhythmic, primitive parts of the brain that do not respond to logic or talk therapy. Drumming does exactly this. It bypasses the prefrontal cortex, the part of you that is trying to be polite and reasonable, and speaks directly to the brainstem.

When you drum, you are engaging in bilateral stimulation. Left hand, right hand. Left hemisphere, right hemisphere. This rhythmic alternating movement helps to process stuck emotions, much like EMDR therapy. It tells your nervous system that you are here, in the present moment, and that you have the power to move the energy that feels so overwhelming. The steady beat provides a predictable, safe container for the unpredictable, chaotic energy of anger. It is a way of holding yourself when the world feels like it is spinning out of control.

Consider the physical mechanics of the strike. The lifting of the arm engages the deltoids and the latissimus dorsi. The downward motion requires the engagement of the core, the rectus abdominis and the obliques. You are not just moving your hands; you are moving your entire upper body. You are forcing the breath out of your lungs with every strike, clearing the stale air that has been trapped in the lower lobes. This is a full-body expression of a full-body emotion.

## The Herbs That Support the Strike

As you prepare to drum, your body might need support. The nervous system, accustomed to holding the tension, might resist the release. This is where the plants come in. They are not garnishes; they are serious medicine. 

Consider a strong infusion of [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20). Skullcap is not a sedative that will numb you out. It is a nervine trophorestorative that feeds the nervous system, helping to ease the spasms in your muscles and the frantic looping in your mind. It allows you to stay present with the anger without being consumed by it. Drink it warm, feeling the bitter, earthy taste ground you in your body. Let the warmth spread down your esophagus and into your stomach, relaxing the vagus nerve.

Another ally is [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20). The name itself tells you what it does: it is the mother's heart. When your heart is racing with unexpressed rage, Motherwort acts as a gentle hand on your chest, reminding you that it is safe to feel this. It is particularly useful when the anger feels like a tight band around your ribs, restricting your breath. A few drops of the tincture under the tongue can act quickly, sending a signal to the heart that it does not need to beat so frantically, that the danger is not immediate, that you are simply processing an old wound.

And then there is [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20). This is the plant for the person who holds their anger in their neck and shoulders, the person who is rigid with the effort of keeping it all together. Blue Vervain is intensely bitter, and it goes straight to the liver, the organ associated with anger in Traditional Chinese Medicine. It helps to move the stagnant liver qi, easing the tension in the upper back and allowing the energy to flow freely again.

## The Practice: Waking the Hands

Find a drum. It does not have to be a professional djembe or a beautifully crafted frame drum, though those are wonderful. It can be a sturdy cardboard box, a plastic bucket, or even your own thighs. The instrument matters less than the intention.

Sit on the floor, feeling your sit bones root into the earth. Notice the tension in your body. Where is the anger living right now? Is it in the tight grip of your levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up to your ears? Is it in the shallow, restricted movement of your diaphragm? 

Take a deep breath, not to calm down, but to make space. 

Begin to tap the drum slowly. Just the right hand. Then the left. 
*Thump. Thump. Thump.*
Do not try to make music. Try to make contact. 

As you strike, imagine the energy of the anger moving down your arms, through your wrists, and out through your fingertips into the drum. Let the drum hold it. The drum can take it. The drum was made for this. Feel the vibration of the strike travel back up your arms, a physical confirmation that you are making an impact, that your actions have resonance in the physical world.

## The Wisdom of the Strike

Resmaa Menakem teaches us about somatic abolitionism, about how trauma and racialized stress are stored in the body. He speaks of the "clean pain" of healing versus the "dirty pain" of avoidance. When we refuse to feel our anger, when we swallow it down because we are afraid of being seen as "too much" or "unhinged," we are choosing dirty pain. We are letting the anger fester, turning it into resentment, depression, or chronic physical pain.

Drumming is a way to choose clean pain. It is a way to face the fire, to let it burn through you, and to emerge on the other side. Menakem emphasizes the importance of settling the nervous system, of finding safety in the body. The steady, predictable rhythm of the drum provides a container for the chaos of the anger. It says: *I am feeling this massive, terrifying emotion, and I am still here. I am still breathing. I am still in rhythm.*

This is not about getting rid of the anger. It is about metabolizing it. It is about taking the raw, unrefined energy of the emotion and digesting it, turning it into fuel for your life. When you drum, you are actively participating in this metabolic process. You are chewing on the anger, breaking it down, and absorbing the nutrients it has to offer - the clarity, the boundaries, the fierce self-protection.

## The Crescendo of Release

As you continue to drum, let the rhythm build. Let it match the intensity of the anger inside you. If the anger is a slow, seething boil, let the drumming be heavy and deliberate. If the anger is a frantic, explosive rage, let the drumming be fast and chaotic. 

Do not hold back. Let your hands fly. Let your breath become ragged. Let the sweat form on your brow. This is the work. This is the alchemy. You are transmuting the lead of unexpressed fury into the gold of vital, life-affirming energy.

Notice what happens in your body as the crescendo peaks. Does the jaw finally release? Do the shoulders drop? Does the breath suddenly deepen, filling the lower lobes of the lungs that have been starved for air? Do you feel a sudden rush of heat in your face, a flush of blood to the skin? This is the vasodilation that comes with the release of tension, the physical evidence that the energy is moving.

When the energy begins to wane, do not stop abruptly. Let the drumming slow down naturally, like a heartbeat returning to a resting pace. 
*Thump... Thump... Thump...*
Until there is only silence.

## The Geography of the Aftermath

Sit in the silence that follows. The air in the room will feel different. Your body will feel different. The dense, vibrating hum of anger may have shifted into a tired, hollow ache, or perhaps a surprising sense of spaciousness. 

Pay attention to the geography of your body now. The masseter muscles, which were locked so tightly just an hour ago, might feel soft, almost bruised. The psoas, the deep core muscle that holds so much of our fear and bracing, might feel elongated, allowing your pelvis to rest more heavily on the floor. The space between your shoulder blades, often a repository for the burdens we carry, might feel lighter, as if a physical weight has been lifted.

This is the moment to tend to yourself. Drink your Skullcap infusion. Wrap a heavy blanket around your shoulders. The body has just done a tremendous amount of work, and it needs to rest. The nervous system is recalibrating, shifting from the sympathetic dominance of the fight-or-flight response back into the parasympathetic state of rest and digest.

You may feel the urge to cry. Let the tears come. They are not tears of weakness; they are the physical manifestation of the release. The body is flushing out the stress hormones, the cortisol and adrenaline that have been coursing through your veins. The tears are literally carrying the chemical residue of the anger out of your body.

## The Ongoing Dialogue

Remember that this is not a one-time fix. The anger will return, because life will continue to cross your boundaries, and your body will continue to alert you. But now, you have a language for it. You have a practice. You have the drum.

When the familiar tightness creeps back into your jaw, when the breath becomes shallow and the hands curl into fists, you do not have to panic. You do not have to swallow it down. You can acknowledge the messenger. You can say, *I hear you. I feel you. Let's move.*

You can return to the drum, to the rhythm, to the physical expression of the truth that lives in your tissues. You can continue the dialogue with your body, a dialogue based on respect, on listening, and on the unwavering belief that your anger is not a flaw, but a vital, necessary part of your humanity.

The drum is always there, waiting for your hands. The rhythm is always there, waiting to be found. The anger is always there, waiting to be heard. And you are always here, capable of holding it all, of moving it all, of transmuting it all.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel silly drumming on a bucket in my living room. How do I get past the embarrassment?**
**A:** The embarrassment is just the prefrontal cortex trying to maintain control. It is the voice of the culture that told you to be quiet and polite. Acknowledge the silliness, and then strike the bucket anyway. The body does not care about looking cool; it cares about moving energy. Once you get into the rhythm, the embarrassment will burn away, replaced by the undeniable reality of the physical sensation.

**Q: What if the drumming makes me more angry?**
**A:** Sometimes, moving the energy brings more of it to the surface. This is normal. If it feels overwhelming, slow the rhythm down. Make it steady and predictable, like a slow heartbeat. Focus on the physical sensation of your hands hitting the surface, rather than the story in your head about why you are angry. If it is still too much, stop, place your hands on your heart and belly, and just breathe. The goal is not to flood the nervous system, but to gently expand its capacity to hold the emotion.

**Q: Can I use drumming for other emotions, like grief or fear?**
**A:** Absolutely. The drum is a versatile vessel. Grief often requires a slower, more mournful rhythm, while fear might need a steady, grounding beat to remind the nervous system that you are safe. The key is to let the body dictate the rhythm, rather than the mind. Listen to what the tissues are asking for, and let your hands follow.

**Q: I live in an apartment and cannot make loud noises. What can I do?**
**A:** You do not need volume to move energy. You can drum on a pillow, which absorbs the sound but still allows for the physical strike. You can also focus on the intensity of the movement rather than the sound. The bilateral stimulation and the physical exertion are what matter most. You can even drum on your own thighs, feeling the impact directly in your own body.

**Q: How often should I do this practice?**
**A:** As often as your body asks for it. Some weeks, you might need to drum every day. Other times, you might go months without needing it. Listen to the tension in your jaw, the tightness in your chest. When the body signals that the anger is stuck, reach for the drum. It is a tool, not a chore. Use it when you need it.

## The Ancestral Echo

When you drum, you are not just moving your own personal anger. You are tapping into an ancestral echo. For thousands of years, across countless cultures, the drum has been the heartbeat of the community. It has been used to call people to war, to celebrate the harvest, to mourn the dead, and to heal the sick. When you strike the skin of the drum, you are joining a lineage of humans who knew that the body needs rhythm to survive.

Think of the women who came before you, the ones who were not allowed to speak their fury, the ones who had to swallow their rage to survive. Their unexpressed anger did not just disappear; it was passed down through the generations, woven into the very DNA of your lineage. When you drum, you are drumming for them, too. You are giving voice to the centuries of silenced women. You are breaking the cycle of suppression.

Feel the weight of that history in your hands. Let it fuel your strikes. Let the rhythm become a bridge between the past and the present, a way of honoring the pain of your ancestors while refusing to carry it forward as a burden. The drum is a time machine, a way of reaching back and offering healing to the ones who could not heal themselves.

## The Physicality of the Strike

Let us look closer at the physicality of the strike. It is not just a movement; it is a complex orchestration of muscle, bone, and fascia. When your hand connects with the drum, the impact sends a shockwave up your arm. This shockwave travels through the carpal bones of the wrist, up the radius and ulna, through the elbow joint, and into the humerus. It reverberates in the shoulder socket, shaking loose the tension that has settled in the rotator cuff.

This physical vibration is profound. It acts as a deep tissue massage from the inside out. It breaks up the adhesions in the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle and organ in your body. When we are chronically angry, our fascia becomes stiff and rigid, like a straightjacket holding us in a state of perpetual bracing. The vibration of the drum helps to melt this rigidity, restoring fluidity and glide to the tissues.

You can feel this happening if you pay close attention. After a few minutes of vigorous drumming, you might notice a tingling sensation in your arms and hands. This is the return of blood flow, the awakening of the nerves. It is the physical sensation of life returning to areas that had been numb and frozen.

## The Breath as the Metronome

As you drum, your breath must become the metronome. Do not hold your breath. Holding the breath is the body's way of bracing against impact, of preparing for a blow. But here, you are the one delivering the blow. You are the active agent, not the passive recipient.

Inhale deeply as you lift your hands, filling the belly, expanding the ribcage in all directions. Exhale forcefully as you strike, letting the breath carry the energy of the anger out of your body. You can even add sound to the exhale - a grunt, a sigh, a shout. The vocal cords are a muscle, too, and they need to release the tension of unspoken words.

When you synchronize the breath with the strike, you are creating a powerful feedback loop in the nervous system. The deep, rhythmic breathing signals to the brain that you are safe, while the forceful strikes allow the sympathetic nervous system to discharge its pent-up energy. It is a perfect balance of activation and regulation.

## The Integration of the Shadow

Carl Jung spoke of the shadow, the parts of ourselves that we hide, deny, or repress because they are deemed unacceptable by society. Anger is often the largest resident of the shadow. We are taught that good people, spiritual people, evolved people do not get angry. And so, we shove our fury into the dark corners of our psyche, where it ferments and turns toxic.

Drumming is a way of inviting the shadow out to play. It is a way of saying, *I see you. I acknowledge your power. I will not hide from you anymore.* When you drum, you are giving the shadow a physical form, a voice, a rhythm. You are integrating the dark, messy, primal parts of yourself into the whole.

This integration is essential for true healing. You cannot be whole if you are constantly at war with a part of yourself. You must learn to embrace the anger, to see it not as a monster to be slain, but as a fierce protector, a guardian of your boundaries. The drum is the mediator in this relationship, the neutral ground where the conscious mind and the shadow can meet and communicate.

## The Return to the World

When the practice is over, when the drum is silent and the body is resting, you must eventually return to the world. You must stand up, walk out of the room, and face the life that triggered the anger in the first place.

But you will not be the same person who sat down. You will be lighter. You will be clearer. The dense fog of unexpressed rage will have lifted, revealing the sharp, clean edges of your truth. You will know what needs to be said, what boundaries need to be drawn, what actions need to be taken.

And you will carry the rhythm with you. Even when you are not holding the drum, you can tap your fingers on your thigh, you can feel the steady beat of your heart, you can remember the power in your hands. You are not defenseless. You are not voiceless. You are a creature of rhythm, a vessel of truth, a force of nature. And your anger is just the beginning.

## The Somatic Vocabulary of Rage

To truly understand the necessity of this practice, we must expand our somatic vocabulary of rage. We must learn to read the subtle and not-so-subtle cues our bodies give us when anger is present but unexpressed. It is rarely just a feeling in the mind; it is a profound physiological event.

Notice the temperature of your skin. Unexpressed anger often manifests as a cold, clammy sweat, particularly on the palms and the back of the neck. This is the body's paradoxical response to a threat it cannot fight or flee from - a state of freeze, where the energy is trapped inside, causing the peripheral blood vessels to constrict. Drumming breaks this freeze. The physical exertion forces the blood vessels to dilate, bringing warmth and color back to the skin. It is a literal thawing of the frozen energy.

Pay attention to your digestion. The enteric nervous system, the complex network of neurons in your gut, is exquisitely sensitive to emotional suppression. When you swallow your anger, your stomach tightens, your digestion slows, and you may experience nausea or a heavy, sinking feeling in your abdomen. This is because the body is diverting energy away from digestion and toward the muscles, preparing for a fight that never happens. The rhythmic, full-body movement of drumming massages the internal organs, stimulating the vagus nerve and encouraging the return of healthy peristalsis. It tells the gut that the danger has passed, that it is safe to digest both food and emotion.

## The Role of the Witness

While drumming can be a solitary practice, there is immense power in being witnessed. If you have a trusted friend, a partner, or a therapist who understands somatic work, invite them to sit with you while you drum. They do not need to do anything. They do not need to fix it, analyze it, or offer advice. They simply need to be a grounded, compassionate presence.

Having a witness changes the alchemy of the practice. It counters the deep-seated shame that often accompanies anger. When someone watches you in your fury, when they see the sweat on your brow and hear the raggedness of your breath, and they do not turn away, they are offering a profound validation. They are saying, *Your anger is acceptable. You are not too much. You belong here, even in your rage.*

If you do not have a human witness, you can invite the natural world to hold that space for you. Drum outside, under the canopy of an old oak tree or beside a running stream. Let the earth absorb the vibration. Let the wind carry the sound. The natural world does not judge anger; it understands storms, earthquakes, and wildfires. It knows that destruction and creation are two sides of the same coin.

## The Alchemy of the Everyday

Ultimately, the goal of this practice is not to become a master drummer, but to become a master of your own somatic experience. It is to take the alchemy of the drum and apply it to the everyday moments of your life.

When your boss dismisses your idea in a meeting, and you feel the familiar tightening in your throat, you do not have to wait until you get home to process it. You can tap your foot under the table. You can press your fingertips into your palms in a steady, rhythmic pulse. You can use the micro-movements of your body to keep the energy flowing, preventing it from getting stuck in the tissues.

When you are stuck in traffic and the frustration is mounting, you can drum on the steering wheel. You can let the rhythm of the turn signal become a metronome for your breath. You can turn the mundane irritations of life into opportunities for somatic release.

This is the true power of the practice. It is not a separate, isolated event that happens only when you are alone in your room. It is a living, breathing relationship with your body, a constant dialogue between your physical sensations and your conscious awareness. It is the realization that you are never truly trapped, because you always have your breath, you always have your body, and you always have the rhythm.
